title: Introduction: Scarcity, Trade-offs and the PPF
summary: Economic foundations: scarcity, opportunity cost, the production possibility frontier (PPF), efficiency, economic models, and positive vs normative analysis.
content_html:
Scarcity, Trade-offs and the Production Possibility Frontier

Economics is the study of the choices people and societies make to attain their unlimited wants given their scarce resources.

Core Concepts

  • Scarcity: The situation in which unlimited wants exceed the limited resources available to fulfill those wants.
  • Resources: Inputs used to produce goods and services -- natural resources (land, water, minerals), labour, capital, and entrepreneurial ability.
  • Trade-off: Because of scarcity, producing more of one good means producing less of another.
  • Opportunity cost: The highest-valued alternative given up when making a choice.

The Production Possibility Frontier (PPF)

The PPF illustrates the trade-offs facing an economy. Points ON the frontier are efficient (maximum output from available resources). Points INSIDE are inefficient (some resources are idle or misused). Points OUTSIDE are currently unattainable.

Example: Tesla can produce 80 sedans and 0 SUVs (Point A), or 0 sedans and 60 SUVs (Point E), or combinations in between. To produce more SUVs, they must sacrifice sedans -- this is the trade-off.

Economic Models

Economists follow these steps to develop a model:

  1. Decide on assumptions.
  2. Formulate a testable hypothesis.
  3. Use economic data to test the hypothesis.
  4. Revise if it fails to explain data.
  5. Retain the revised model for similar questions.

Positive vs Normative Analysis

  • Positive analysis: Concerned with what IS -- value-free statements that can be checked against facts. Example: 'An increase in the minimum wage reduces employment by 3%.'
  • Normative analysis: Concerned with what OUGHT TO BE -- involves value judgements that cannot be tested. Example: 'The government should increase the minimum wage.'

Microeconomics vs Macroeconomics

  • Microeconomics: The study of how households and firms make choices, how they interact in markets, and how government attempts to influence their choices.
  • Macroeconomics: The study of the economy as a whole -- inflation, unemployment, economic growth.

exercises.title: Economics definition
exercises.prompt: Economics is best defined as the study of:
options: How governments make monetary policy
The choices people and societies make given scarce resources to attain unlimited wants
How to maximise corporate profits
The history of money and trade
exercises.answer: The choices people and societies make given scarce resources to attain unlimited wants

exercises.title: PPF efficient point
exercises.prompt: A point ON the production possibility frontier represents:
options: An unattainable combination
An inefficient combination with idle resources
An efficient combination using maximum available resources
A future target output level
exercises.answer: An efficient combination using maximum available resources

exercises.title: PPF inside point
exercises.prompt: A point INSIDE the PPF indicates:
options: The economy is producing beyond its capacity
Resources are not being fully or efficiently used
The economy is growing rapidly
Opportunity costs are zero
exercises.answer: Resources are not being fully or efficiently used

exercises.title: Positive vs normative
exercises.prompt: Which statement is an example of POSITIVE economic analysis?
options: The government should reduce income taxes.
The unemployment rate is 5.2% this quarter.
Workers deserve a living wage.
Australia should increase foreign aid spending.
exercises.answer: The unemployment rate is 5.2% this quarter.

exercises.title: Opportunity cost
exercises.prompt: Opportunity cost is best defined as:
options: The financial cost of producing a good
The highest-valued alternative given up when making a choice
The total cost of all resources used in production
The price a consumer pays for a good
exercises.answer: The highest-valued alternative given up when making a choice

exercises.title: Scarcity definition
options: Only rare goods have economic value
There is not enough of anything for anyone
Unlimited wants exceed the limited resources available
Governments must ration all goods
exercises.answer: Unlimited wants exceed the limited resources available

exercises.title: Microeconomics focus
options: National inflation rates
How households and firms make choices and interact in markets
Aggregate economic growth
Government fiscal deficits
exercises.answer: How households and firms make choices and interact in markets

exercises.title: Economic model purpose
exercises.prompt: An economic model is best compared to a:
options: Precise replica of reality
Map -- a simplified guide to reality
Computer simulation of all transactions
Statistical database of prices
exercises.answer: Map -- a simplified guide to reality
